message: This code uses Node.js 'fs' APIs to read files. If you are running on the Bun runtime, prefer using the faster, optimized native API 'Bun.file(path)' which provides '.text()', '.json()', and stream interfaces directly.

This code uses Node.js 'fs' APIs to read files. If you are running on the Bun runtime, prefer using the faster, optimized native API 'Bun.file(path)' which provides '.text()', '.json()', and stream interfaces directly.

This code uses external hashing libraries (bcrypt, bcryptjs, argon2) or Node.js crypto.pbkdf2 for password hashing. If you are running on the Bun runtime, prefer using the optimized native APIs 'Bun.password.hash(...)' and 'Bun.password.verify(...)'.

This code uses external hashing libraries (bcrypt, bcryptjs, argon2) or Node.js crypto.pbkdf2 for password hashing. If you are running on the Bun runtime, prefer using the optimized native APIs 'Bun.password.hash(...)' and 'Bun.password.verify(...)'.

This code uses external hashing libraries (bcrypt, bcryptjs, argon2) or Node.js crypto.pbkdf2 for password hashing. If you are running on the Bun runtime, prefer using the optimized native APIs 'Bun.password.hash(...)' and 'Bun.password.verify(...)'.

This code uses external hashing libraries (bcrypt, bcryptjs, argon2) or Node.js crypto.pbkdf2 for password hashing. If you are running on the Bun runtime, prefer using the optimized native APIs 'Bun.password.hash(...)' and 'Bun.password.verify(...)'.
This code builds an HTTP server using Node's 'http.createServer'. If you are running on the Bun runtime, prefer using the optimized native API 'Bun.serve(...)' for significantly higher performance.
This code uses Node.js 'fs' APIs to write files. If you are running on the Bun runtime, prefer using the faster, optimized native API 'Bun.write(path, data)' for better performance.

Bun shell command is built from a dynamic value. If the value is user-controlled, shell metacharacters can change the command that runs. Use a fixed command with an argument array, validate against an allowlist, or avoid invoking a shell.

Bun shell command is built from a dynamic value. If the value is user-controlled, shell metacharacters can change the command that runs. Use a fixed command with an argument array, validate against an allowlist, or avoid invoking a shell.

bun:sqlite query is built with string concatenation or interpolation. If any part of the value is user-controlled, this can become SQL injection. Use prepared statements with placeholders and bind values separately.

bun:sqlite query is built with string concatenation or interpolation. If any part of the value is user-controlled, this can become SQL injection. Use prepared statements with placeholders and bind values separately.

An asynchronous Express route handler does not wrap its execution in a try/catch block or use an async error handler wrapper. Unhandled async rejections in Express can crash the Node.js process or cause requests to hang. Wrap the body in try/catch and pass the error to next(err).

An asynchronous Express route handler does not wrap its execution in a try/catch block or use an async error handler wrapper. Unhandled async rejections in Express can crash the Node.js process or cause requests to hang. Wrap the body in try/catch and pass the error to next(err).

Express body parsing middleware is configured without an explicit payload limit. Large JSON or form bodies can consume memory and CPU before route handlers run, creating a simple denial-of-service path. Set a small application-specific limit such as express.json({ limit: "1mb" }).

Express body parsing middleware is configured without an explicit payload limit. Large JSON or form bodies can consume memory and CPU before route handlers run, creating a simple denial-of-service path. Set a small application-specific limit such as express.json({ limit: "1mb" }).

CORS is configured with origin '*' and credentials enabled. Browsers reject this combination, and relaxing it later often turns into credential leakage. Use an explicit allowlist of trusted origins when cookies or authorization headers are allowed.

CORS is configured with origin '*' and credentials enabled. Browsers reject this combination, and relaxing it later often turns into credential leakage. Use an explicit allowlist of trusted origins when cookies or authorization headers are allowed.

express-session config weakens the session cookie security flags. Session cookies should normally be httpOnly so JavaScript cannot read them, and secure in production so they are not sent over plain HTTP. Keep httpOnly enabled and set secure true behind HTTPS.

express-session config weakens the session cookie security flags. Session cookies should normally be httpOnly so JavaScript cannot read them, and secure in production so they are not sent over plain HTTP. Keep httpOnly enabled and set secure true behind HTTPS.

The Express application is initialized but does not appear to use the 'helmet' middleware. Helmet sets critical security headers to protect the app from common web vulnerabilities (XSS, Clickjacking, MIME-sniffing, etc.). Add app.use(helmet()) to secure HTTP headers.

The Express application is initialized but does not appear to use the 'helmet' middleware. Helmet sets critical security headers to protect the app from common web vulnerabilities (XSS, Clickjacking, MIME-sniffing, etc.). Add app.use(helmet()) to secure HTTP headers.

This Express route handler does not appear to be protected by a rate-limiter. Without rate limiting, endpoints are vulnerable to brute-force and denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks. Apply express-rate-limit (or a similar middleware) to this router or globally via app.use(rateLimit({ ... })).

A sensitive Express route (e.g., containing 'admin', 'delete', 'update', 'secure', 'private', or 'remove') does not seem to use any authorization or authentication middleware. Ensure that route handlers for sensitive paths are protected using an authentication or permission-checking middleware.

A sensitive Express route (e.g., containing 'admin', 'delete', 'update', 'secure', 'private', or 'remove') does not seem to use any authorization or authentication middleware. Ensure that route handlers for sensitive paths are protected using an authentication or permission-checking middleware.

An await sits directly inside a loop, so the iterations run strictly one after another: each waits for the previous to finish. When the operations are independent (e.g. fetching N urls), this is needlessly slow and should be parallelized with Promise.all(items.map(...)). If each iteration genuinely depends on the previous, or you are intentionally rate-limiting, the serial form is correct.

bcrypt is configured with a cost factor below 10. Low bcrypt rounds make offline password cracking materially cheaper if password hashes leak. Use a cost of at least 10, then tune upward based on your production latency budget.

Synchronous cryptographic operations (e.g. pbkdf2Sync, scryptSync, bcrypt.hashSync, compareSync) run inside a request handler. CPU-intensive sync crypto blocks the single-threaded Node.js event loop, preventing all other concurrent requests from being processed and creating a simple denial-of-service (DoS) vulnerability. Use asynchronous alternatives like bcrypt.hash or crypto.pbkdf2.

Synchronous cryptographic operations (e.g. pbkdf2Sync, scryptSync, bcrypt.hashSync, compareSync) run inside a request handler. CPU-intensive sync crypto blocks the single-threaded Node.js event loop, preventing all other concurrent requests from being processed and creating a simple denial-of-service (DoS) vulnerability. Use asynchronous alternatives like bcrypt.hash or crypto.pbkdf2.
innerHTML is assigned a dynamic value. If that value contains user-controlled data, this is a DOM XSS sink. Prefer textContent for plain text, or sanitize trusted HTML before assigning it.
innerHTML is assigned a dynamic value. If that value contains user-controlled data, this is a DOM XSS sink. Prefer textContent for plain text, or sanitize trusted HTML before assigning it.
innerHTML is assigned a dynamic value. If that value contains user-controlled data, this is a DOM XSS sink. Prefer textContent for plain text, or sanitize trusted HTML before assigning it.
Dynamic code execution through eval()/Function() can execute attacker-controlled input as JavaScript. Avoid interpreting strings as code; use explicit parsing or a constrained dispatch table instead.

This catch block is empty, so the error is silently swallowed: failures vanish with no log, no rethrow, and no recovery, which hides bugs and makes incidents undebuggable. Handle the error, log it, or rethrow. If ignoring is truly intentional, leave a comment explaining why.

fetch() is called from inside a loop. This often creates an N+1 network pattern or forces requests to run one by one when combined with await. Prefer batching, a backend bulk endpoint, or Promise.all over a mapped collection when the requests are independent.

A hardcoded HTTP/HTTPS URL is used directly in a network request. To ensure flexibility across different environments (development, staging, production), avoid hardcoding base URLs. Load the base URL from an environment variable or use an API client with a configured baseURL.

A hardcoded secret or credential appears in the source (an AWS access key, a GitHub/Slack/Stripe token, a Google API key, or a PEM private key). Committed secrets are exposed to everyone with repository access and stay in git history even after the line is deleted. Move the value to an environment variable or a secret manager, rotate the leaked credential immediately, and purge it from git history.

This throws a "not implemented" placeholder, so the function is an unfinished stub left in the codebase: if a real caller reaches it, it fails at runtime. Implement the function, or remove it if it is dead code. A genuine "subclasses must override" placeholder belongs on an abstract method, not a shipped concrete path.

document.cookie is assigned without an obvious Secure attribute. Client-set cookies are also readable from JavaScript, so auth material should normally be set server-side with HttpOnly, Secure, and SameSite. If this is not auth material, still confirm the cookie is intentionally accessible to scripts.
A javascript: URL executes code when the link is activated. Use a real route, button handler, or safe URL instead.

JSON.parse is called on request-controlled input without a local try/catch. A malformed payload can throw synchronously and turn a single bad request into a 500 response or process-level failure, depending on the framework error path. Validate the input or catch SyntaxError before using it.

JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(...)) is used as a deep clone. This is slow on large objects and silently changes values such as Date, Map, Set, undefined, NaN, Infinity, RegExp, functions, and class instances. Prefer structuredClone where available, or use a clone strategy that preserves the data shape you actually need.

A JWT is read with jwt.decode(), which only base64-decodes the token and does NOT check the signature. Any value derived from a decoded-but-unverified token (user id, role, expiry) is attacker-controlled: a forged token passes. Use jwt.verify(token, secret) before trusting the payload. decode() is only safe for reading the header to pick a key, never for authorization decisions.

A console.log/console.debug call was left in the source. These are typically debugging leftovers (a common artifact of AI-generated or rushed code) that leak data to the browser console and add noise in production. Remove it, or route it through an intentional logger. console.warn/console.error are not flagged, since those are usually deliberate.

Lodash is imported from the top-level package, which can pull a much larger bundle than the one helper this file needs. Prefer a per-method import such as lodash/debounce, or use lodash-es with a bundler that tree-shakes it correctly.

Lodash is imported from the top-level package, which can pull a much larger bundle than the one helper this file needs. Prefer a per-method import such as lodash/debounce, or use lodash-es with a bundler that tree-shakes it correctly.

Math.random() is used to create a token, nonce, secret, session value, or other security-sensitive identifier. Math.random() is not cryptographically secure and can be predictable. Use crypto.getRandomValues(), crypto.randomUUID(), or a server-side CSPRNG instead.

A message event handler does not appear to check event.origin. postMessage data can come from untrusted origins, so handlers should verify the sender against a strict allowlist before trusting event.data.

This file mixes ESM imports with CommonJS require(). Mixed module systems often behave differently across Node, bundlers, and test runners, especially under "type": "module". Use one module system consistently in a file, or isolate interop behind a small adapter.

The faker mock-data library is imported in non-test code, so fake/random data can be shipped into a production path instead of real data. Move faker behind tests/fixtures/seed scripts, or replace it with a real data source.

A promise is started as a standalone statement without await, return, or a rejection handler. If it rejects, Node reports an unhandled rejection and modern runtimes may terminate the process. Await/return it, or attach an explicit catch handler.

Unsafe raw SQL query detected. Using string interpolation or concatenation inside Prisma ($queryRaw, $executeRaw, $queryRawUnsafe, $executeRawUnsafe) or TypeORM (query) bypasses parameterized query generation, risking SQL injection. Use safe tagged templates (e.g. prisma.$queryRaw`...`) or pass parameters as a separate argument/binding.

Request-controlled input is joined into a filesystem path without an obvious basename/normalize/allowlist step. Attackers can use ../ segments or absolute paths to escape the intended directory. Resolve against a fixed base directory and reject paths that leave it, or map user input to known filenames.

This comment looks like an unfinished placeholder or AI-generation artifact (e.g. "your code here", "rest of the implementation", "TODO: implement", "as an AI ..."). It usually means code was elided or never written, so the surrounding function may be incomplete or non-functional. Replace it with the real implementation, or remove the comment if the code is actually complete.

It usually means code was elided or never written, so the surrounding function may be incomplete or non-functional. Replace it with the real implementation, or remove the comment if the code is actually complete.

Direct access to 'process.env' detected outside of configuration files. To prevent undefined runtime variables and ensure clean architecture, centralize all environment variable access in a dedicated configuration file (e.g., config.ts or config.js) and import the configuration instead.

This reduce callback rebuilds the accumulator with [...acc, ...] on every iteration, which copies the growing array repeatedly and turns a linear collection step into O(n²) work. Prefer map/filter, or mutate a local accumulator inside the reduce callback when reduce is genuinely needed.

Regular expression contains nested quantifiers (e.g. (a+)+, ([a-z]+)*) which can lead to Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDoS) due to catastrophic backtracking when matched against malicious or extremely long inputs. Simplify the regex or use a safe regex engine/validator.

Regular expression contains nested quantifiers (e.g. (a+)+, ([a-z]+)*) which can lead to Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDoS) due to catastrophic backtracking when matched against malicious or extremely long inputs. Simplify the regex or use a safe regex engine/validator.

Regular expression contains nested quantifiers (e.g. (a+)+, ([a-z]+)*) which can lead to Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDoS) due to catastrophic backtracking when matched against malicious or extremely long inputs. Simplify the regex or use a safe regex engine/validator.

A token/secret/credential is written to localStorage or sessionStorage. Web storage is readable by any script on the page, so any XSS can exfiltrate it; unlike an httpOnly cookie it is not protected from JavaScript. Prefer an httpOnly, Secure, SameSite cookie for auth material, or keep it in memory.

Synchronous fs I/O runs inside an Express request handler. readFileSync, writeFileSync, and existsSync block the Node.js event loop, so one slow disk operation can stall unrelated requests. Use async fs APIs, pre-load static data at startup, or move the work outside the request path.

Synchronous fs I/O runs inside an Express request handler. readFileSync, writeFileSync, and existsSync block the Node.js event loop, so one slow disk operation can stall unrelated requests. Use async fs APIs, pre-load static data at startup, or move the work outside the request path.

User-controlled request data reaches an OS command execution sink. An attacker can inject shell metacharacters to run arbitrary commands on the server. Avoid passing request data into a shell; use a fixed command with an argument array (no shell), or validate the input against a strict allowlist before execution.

Browser-controlled input reaches an HTML-injection sink without sanitization, leading to DOM-based Cross-Site Scripting (XSS). An attacker can craft a URL, fragment, or request field that injects script into the page. Render untrusted data as text (textContent), or sanitize it with a trusted library (e.g. DOMPurify.sanitize) before assigning it to an HTML sink.

A hardcoded secret or credential (an AWS access key, a GitHub/Slack/Stripe token, a Google API key, or a PEM private key) is assigned to a variable or object property and then flows into a function call. Unlike the regex rule, this catches indirect usage where the literal is stored first and consumed elsewhere. Committed secrets are exposed to everyone with repository access and remain in git history even after deletion. Move the value to an environment variable or a secret manager, rotate the leaked credential immediately, and purge it from git history.

User-controlled request data reaches an outbound HTTP request URL. This can lead to Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF), where attackers make the server call internal services or attacker-controlled hosts. Resolve the destination from trusted route names or validate it against a strict allowlist before making the request.

@ts-ignore suppresses the TypeScript checker without documenting why. This can hide real type regressions permanently. Prefer @ts-expect-error with a short reason, or add an explicit reason if the suppression is unavoidable.

DTO class property is defined without any decorators. In NestJS, properties inside DTO classes (e.g. classes ending in Dto) must have at least one validation decorator (such as @IsString(), @IsNumber(), etc.) for the global ValidationPipe to validate them. Properties without decorators are ignored by validation, allowing unchecked payload fields.

A NestJS controller directly performs database or ORM operations. NestJS architecture mandates that controllers only handle HTTP routing and delegate business logic and data access to services. Move this database operation into a service class.

Heavy library is statically imported. Statically importing large libraries (like monaco-editor, three, lodash, or chart.js) increases the initial client bundle size and slows down page loads. Consider using dynamic imports (e.g., Next.js dynamic() or dynamic import()) to load this library lazily.

Client Components cannot be async functions. Making a Client Component async results in runtime errors and prevents proper client-side rendering. Convert the component to a synchronous function and use hooks like useEffect or useSWR/TanStack Query for data fetching, or keep it as a Server Component.

Convert the component to a synchronous function and use hooks like useEffect or useSWR/TanStack Query for data fetching, or keep it as a Server Component.

A Next.js Server Component or route file uses a browser-only API without a "use client" directive. window, document, localStorage, sessionStorage, navigator, and similar APIs are not available during server rendering and can crash with ReferenceError. Move this code to a Client Component or guard it so it only runs in the browser.

Client-side data fetching detected inside useEffect in a Client Component. Using useEffect with fetch or axios in Client Components can lead to request waterfalls, increased bundle sizes, and negative impact on SEO. Consider moving data fetching to a React Server Component (RSC), or use a library like TanStack Query for client-side state and caching.

A cookie is set via cookies().set() without explicitly enabling the secure flag. Sensitive cookies (like session tokens) should always be set with 'secure: true' in production to prevent them from being transmitted over unencrypted HTTP connections.

A cookie is set via cookies().set() without explicitly enabling the secure flag. Sensitive cookies (like session tokens) should always be set with 'secure: true' in production to prevent them from being transmitted over unencrypted HTTP connections.

Calling 'redirect()' or 'permanentRedirect()' inside a try-catch block can prevent the redirect from working correctly because Next.js uses thrown errors internally to control routing. Call redirect() outside the try-catch block or ensure the redirect error is rethrown.

A standard HTML tag is used. In Next.js, using bypasses automatic image optimization (lazy loading, responsive sizing, and modern format conversion). Use the '' component from 'next/image' instead.

A standard HTML <a> tag is used with an internal route. In Next.js, using <a> for internal navigation causes a full page reload, losing client-side routing benefits. Use the '<Link>' component from 'next/link' instead.

A NEXT_PUBLIC_* environment variable named like a secret (secret, token, password, credential, private/api key) is read here. Next.js inlines every NEXT_PUBLIC_* value into the client JavaScript bundle, so this value is shipped to the browser and readable by anyone. If it is a real secret, drop the NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix and read it only in server code (route handlers, server components, getServerSideProps). If the value is genuinely public (e.g. a publishable key), rename it so it no longer looks like a secret.

If the value is genuinely public (e.g. a publishable key), rename it so it no longer looks like a secret.

A Next.js request API is called at module scope. cookies(), headers(), and similar request-bound APIs force the route or component to be dynamic and can disable static optimization for the whole file. Move the call inside the handler/component that actually needs request data.

A Next.js App Router route handler exports a function whose name is not a valid HTTP method. Next.js only recognizes GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, HEAD, and OPTIONS in route files; other exported handler names are ignored and the endpoint will not respond as intended.

A Next.js Server Action accepts input but does not appear to validate it with a schema parser. Server Actions are callable from the client, so unvalidated parameters can become authorization, injection, or data integrity bugs. Validate the input with Zod, Valibot, or a similar schema before using it.

Server Component fetches its own API route. Server Components can access databases, services, or internal APIs directly in Next.js. Fetching an internal /api/... route adds unnecessary HTTP request overhead (serialization, latency, network hop). Call the database or service function directly instead of performing an HTTP fetch.

A client component (using "use client") imports a server-only package or Node.js core module. This will fail to run or bundle in the browser. Ensure this import is only used in server components, or mock/stub the functionality on the client.

Server-side fetch() is called with a dynamic or unvalidated URL. If this URL is built from user-controlled input, it can lead to Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF), allowing attackers to query internal services, bypass firewalls, or leak credentials. Validate the URL against a strict allowlist of allowed domains, or ensure the input is sanitized and mapped to safe endpoints.

Unnecessary "use client" directive at the top of the file. No React/Next.js hooks, event handlers, client-only APIs (like createContext), or browser globals (like window/document) were detected. Removing "use client" allows this component to be rendered as a React Server Component (RSC), which reduces the client-side bundle size and improves initial page load performance.

A Next.js redirect target is built from a dynamic value. If this value can be influenced by a user, the app may redirect to an attacker-controlled URL. Validate against an allowlist, resolve only internal paths, or build the destination from trusted route names.

'useSearchParams()' is used in a Client Component. Next.js requires components using 'useSearchParams()' to be wrapped in a '<Suspense>' boundary at the parent level during static rendering. Without a Suspense boundary, the entire page will fallback to client-side rendering.

dangerouslySetInnerHTML is fed a dynamic value (not a string literal). If that value contains any user-controlled data, this is a stored/reflected XSS sink. Confirm the HTML is sanitized (e.g. DOMPurify) before it reaches this prop, or render the value as text instead.

Direct DOM manipulation call detected in React file. Accessing or modifying the DOM directly (using document.getElementById, querySelector, etc.) bypasses the Virtual DOM, causing state inconsistencies and rendering issues. Use React refs (useRef) or state binding instead.

A function component defines defaultProps. React 19 removed defaultProps support for function components. Use JavaScript default parameters or destructuring defaults so the fallback still applies after upgrading.

A function component defines propTypes. React 19 ignores propTypes on function components, so this runtime validation no longer protects the component contract. Move the contract to TypeScript types or validate untrusted data at the application boundary with a schema.
This legacy ReactDOM API is removed or unsupported by modern React roots. Use createRoot(...).render(...), hydrateRoot(...), root.unmount(), or a ref-based alternative instead.
This legacy ReactDOM API is removed or unsupported by modern React roots. Use createRoot(...).render(...), hydrateRoot(...), root.unmount(), or a ref-based alternative instead.

forwardRef is no longer necessary in React 19 because function components can receive ref as a normal prop. React documents forwardRef for future deprecation. Prefer a ref prop for new React 19 code and migrate when the component API can change safely.

forwardRef is no longer necessary in React 19 because function components can receive ref as a normal prop. React documents forwardRef for future deprecation. Prefer a ref prop for new React 19 code and migrate when the component API can change safely.

A React key derived from Math.random()/Date.now() changes on every render, so React remounts the element each time: local state is lost, inputs lose focus, and reconciliation degrades. Use a stable id from the data instead.

FlatList renderItem receives an inline function. This creates a new function on every render, which can defeat list item memoization and add avoidable work on large lists. Move renderItem to a stable function or wrap it in useCallback with correct dependencies.

FlatList renderItem receives an inline function. This creates a new function on every render, which can defeat list item memoization and add avoidable work on large lists. Move renderItem to a stable function or wrap it in useCallback with correct dependencies.

React Native AsyncStorage is used to persist a token, secret, credential, or auth value. AsyncStorage is not encrypted storage, so sensitive values can be recovered from device backups, rooted devices, or local compromise. Store auth material in SecureStore, Keychain, Keystore, or another platform-backed secure storage API.

React Native AsyncStorage is used to persist a token, secret, credential, or auth value. AsyncStorage is not encrypted storage, so sensitive values can be recovered from device backups, rooted devices, or local compromise. Store auth material in SecureStore, Keychain, Keystore, or another platform-backed secure storage API.

A DOM element is rendered inside a React Native component. React Native does not support HTML tags such as div, span, p, button, or input; this crashes or fails to render on native platforms. Use View, Text, Pressable, TextInput, or another React Native component instead.

A DOM element is rendered inside a React Native component. React Native does not support HTML tags such as div, span, p, button, or input; this crashes or fails to render on native platforms. Use View, Text, Pressable, TextInput, or another React Native component instead.

In React Native, `{value && <JSX/>}` renders a bare 0 when value is 0 — a text node outside <Text> — which crashes the screen ("Text strings must be rendered within a <Text> component"). A numeric left operand (.length or a count-like variable) hits this whenever the list/value is empty. Guard with `value > 0 && ...`, `!!value && ...`, or a ternary returning null.

In React Native, `{value && <JSX/>}` renders a bare 0 when value is 0 — a text node outside <Text> — which crashes the screen ("Text strings must be rendered within a <Text> component"). A numeric left operand (.length or a count-like variable) hits this whenever the list/value is empty. Guard with `value > 0 && ...`, `!!value && ...`, or a ternary returning null.

ScrollView renders every child eagerly while this code maps over a dynamic collection. Large or growing lists can cause memory pressure and jank on native devices. Use FlatList or SectionList so React Native can virtualize rows.

Server Action accepts input but does not appear to perform any authorization or authentication check. Server Actions are publicly accessible POST endpoints. Always verify the user's session and permissions at the start of the action (e.g. using auth(), getServerSession(), or custom verification helpers) to prevent unauthorized access.

Calling 'redirect()' or 'notFound()' inside a try-catch block can prevent the navigation from working correctly because TanStack Router uses thrown errors internally to redirect or show a 404 page. Call redirect() outside the try-catch block or rethrow the exception.

useQuery is executing an HTTP write operation. Queries are designed for idempotent reads and may refetch automatically; POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE belong in useMutation so retries, pending state, and cache updates follow mutation semantics.

useQuery is executing an HTTP write operation. Queries are designed for idempotent reads and may refetch automatically; POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE belong in useMutation so retries, pending state, and cache updates follow mutation semantics.

This TanStack Query queryFn has a block body but returns no value. Query functions must resolve data; resolving undefined breaks the cache contract and is rejected by current TanStack Query versions. Return the fetched value or throw an error.

QueryClient is created inside a React component or hook, so a new cache can be created on each render. Create it once at module scope, or lazily store it in useState/useRef before passing it to QueryClientProvider.

useMutation is called without cache invalidation or update. When performing a mutation, you should invalidate relevant query caches using queryClient.invalidateQueries() or update query data using queryClient.setQueryData() in onSuccess or onSettled callbacks. Otherwise, the user interface will remain stale and won't reflect the server-side changes.

TanStack Query key contains a non-serializable value. Functions, Date, Map, Set, or class instances can make cache identity unstable or hard to invalidate consistently. Use primitive values or plain serializable objects in query keys.

TanStack Query key contains a non-serializable value. Functions, Date, Map, Set, or class instances can make cache identity unstable or hard to invalidate consistently. Use primitive values or plain serializable objects in query keys.

TanStack Query key uses an inline string literal. Repeating raw strings across useQuery, setQueryData, and invalidateQueries makes cache updates easy to mistype or drift. Prefer a query-key factory or shared constant so reads and invalidations use the same key shape.

useQuery is called without specifying a staleTime. By default, staleTime is 0, which means queries are instantly considered stale. For server-side rendered (SSR) or prefetched data, this causes an immediate client-side refetch upon hydration, resulting in duplicate network requests and potential screen flickering. Set a staleTime (e.g. staleTime: 1000 * 60 * 5) to cache and reuse the hydrated data.

Vite dev server is configured to listen on all interfaces. This can expose local development services to the LAN or container network. Bind to localhost unless remote device testing is intentional, and document the reason when host: true is required.

process.env should not be used in a Vite project. Vite does not inject process.env by default, which can cause runtime ReferenceErrors (process is not defined) in the browser. Use import.meta.env instead.

A VITE_* environment variable is named like a secret. Vite exposes every VITE_* value to client-side code, so tokens, passwords, private keys, and credentials with this prefix are shipped to the browser. Rename real secrets without the VITE_ prefix and read them only on the server.
